sourceFiles.name: .fixed-models/vehicle-model__review-2026-03-25T12-41-48-087Z__selected-5-issues__2026-03-25T12-45-57-462Z.sysml

fix.title: Make engine specific power dimensionally explicit in kW/kg and constrain it to an automotive range
fix.description: This fix resolves the plausibility defect by replacing the unitless engine power-to-mass comparison with explicitly typed quantity attributes and a kW/kg-based specific-power constraint suitable for an automotive engine. It introduces quantity kinds and units for power, mass, displacement, torque, rotational speed, vehicle speed, acceleration time, and braking deceleration so the model is dimensionally grounded. It also updates the engine constraint and the performance requirement to use a declared `specificPower` attribute in kW/kg, preventing ambiguous interpretation of thresholds and making propulsion plausibility analysable.

changes.description: Replaced unitless scalar physical attributes with explicit quantity-value structures carrying a numeric value and declared unit, then updated all dependent constraints and instance values to use `.value` and fixed unit declarations.
Added a dimensionally explicit engine-specific-power attribute and definition so the model computes and constrains propulsion plausibility in kW/kg rather than with an ambiguous unitless threshold.

Updated the vehicle performance requirement to reference the explicit engine specific power unit and value, preserving the vehicle-level acceleration check while making the propulsion basis physically grounded.

results.name: Contextual Plausibility
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A passenger road vehicle model intended to represent a car with engine, transmission, wheels, and top-level performance/safety requirements for driving, acceleration, and braking.
- Operational domain: Automotive / road vehicle.
- Lifecycle phase: Early concept to preliminary design. The model contains coarse architecture, simple constraints, and example instantiated values, but not detailed design data.
- Model scope: A partial system model / illustrative fragment with a single example vehicle instance (`VehicleModel::myCar`).
- Known scope limitations: The model does not include units, environment definitions, detailed brake system architecture, thermal systems, electrical systems, suspension/structure, fuel/energy storage, control software, communications, maintenance concept, or external infrastructure assumptions. Checks depending on these omitted elements are skipped where appropriate rather than treated as defects.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Physical Plausibility  -  The explicit numeric values present for vehicle mass, wheel size, friction coefficient, transmission efficiency, braking deceleration, and example performance are broadly plausible for a conventional passenger car, and no explicit law-of-physics violation is stated in the provided values.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Timing & Sequencing Plausibility  -  No timing architecture, computational platform, startup sequencing, watchdogs, human response times, or event ordering are modelled, so there is no explicit implausible timing claim to assess in this fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Environmental Plausibility  -  No operating environment, temperature, humidity, vibration, altitude, EMC, or lifetime exposure assumptions are specified, so the model does not make any explicit environmentally implausible claim within its stated scope.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Operational Plausibility  -  The operational concept is limited to a generic `DriveCar` use case and does not specify staffing, maintenance, uptime, or logistics assumptions that would create an explicit operational plausibility conflict.

CHECK PASSED: 5. External System & Environment Assumptions  -  The model does not depend on external services such as GPS, communications, grid power, or partner-system timing/availability assumptions, so no implausible external dependency is asserted.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-001]
Element:        VehicleModel::Engine::powerMassRatio
Check:          6. Architectural Plausibility
Finding:        The engine feasibility constraint is dimensionally underspecified and therefore not physically grounded: `power / mass > 0.1` omits units, allowing the same numeric threshold to be interpreted in incompatible ways (e.g., kW/kg, hp/kg, W/kg). This weakens the plausibility of any architectural decision based on propulsion capability because the model cannot distinguish realistic from unrealistic engine specific power.
Evidence:       `constraint powerMassRatio { power / mass > 0.1 }` with `attribute power : Real;` and `attribute mass : Real;`
Domain Basis:   Physical performance constraints must be dimensionally consistent and use declared units; in vehicle engineering, specific power is meaningful only with an explicit unit basis.
Score:          0.68  -  Moderate defect because plausibility judgments about engine capability are not reliably analysable from unitless quantities.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Reviewers may accept an infeasible engine or reject a feasible one depending on assumed units, leading to incorrect architectural trade studies.
Recommendation: Add explicit quantity kinds and units for `power` and `mass`, then restate the constraint with a justified automotive-specific power range (for example in kW/kg) tied to the intended engine class.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-002]
Element:        VehicleModel::PerformanceReq
Check:          6. Architectural Plausibility
Finding:        The acceleration requirement uses a surrogate ratio `engine.power * transmission.efficiency / vehicle.mass >= 0.18` without modelling drivetrain traction limits, rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, gear-dependent wheel torque, or speed range. As written, it can certify 0-60 mph feasibility from insufficient architectural evidence and may treat a numerically convenient ratio as proof of achievable acceleration.
Evidence:       `require constraint { vehicle.zeroToSixtyTime < 6.0 and vehicle.engine.power * vehicle.transmission.efficiency / vehicle.mass >= 0.18 }`
Domain Basis:   Real vehicle acceleration is governed by wheel tractive effort versus resistive forces across the speed profile, not by power-to-mass ratio alone; power-to-mass can be a screening heuristic but is not by itself a closed feasibility demonstration.
Score:          0.58  -  Moderate defect because the requirement may support an incorrect design decision about performance feasibility under real driving conditions.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  The concern depends on standard automotive engineering practice and the intended fidelity of this early-phase model; if the ratio is explicitly intended only as a coarse heuristic, the issue is less severe.
Impact:         The model may falsely show compliance with acceleration targets even when the drivetrain cannot actually deliver the required wheel force over the launch and mid-speed regime.
Recommendation: Replace or supplement the surrogate with a simplified but causal vehicle longitudinal dynamics model including wheel radius, traction limit, gear ratio usage, and resistive forces, or explicitly mark the current relation as a coarse screening approximation.

ISSUE [PLAUSIBILITY-003]
Element:        VehicleModel::SafetyReq
Check:          7. Causal Plausibility
Finding:        The braking requirement asserts vehicle-level deceleration and per-wheel brake torque capacity but does not model the causal path connecting them, such as wheel radius, normal load distribution, brake balance, tire-road force generation, or brake system actuation. Without that feedback/force path, the model claims braking performance without a physically realisable causal demonstration.
Evidence:       `require constraint { vehicle.brakingDeceleration >= 8.0 and vehicle.wheels[1].brakeTorqueCapacity >= 1500.0 ... and vehicle.wheels[4].tireRoadFrictionCoefficient >= 0.8 }`
Domain Basis:   Vehicle deceleration arises from tire longitudinal force created by brake torque through wheel geometry and available normal load; brake torque capacity alone is not causally equivalent to achieved deceleration.
Score:          0.60  -  Moderate defect because the model links cause and effect only implicitly, reducing confidence that the claimed braking performance is physically demonstrable.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  The model is clearly simplified, and the missing causal chain may be intentionally abstracted at concept level, but the current requirement still overstates what is proven.
Impact:         Safety performance could be accepted without enough evidence that the wheel/brake configuration can actually generate and sustain the required deceleration.
Recommendation: Add causal relations from brake torque to tire force to vehicle deceleration, including wheel effective radius and at least a simplified axle load/traction model, or qualify the requirement as a high-level placeholder pending dynamics analysis.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Causal Plausibility  -  Aside from the braking-performance abstraction noted above, the model does not contain explicit causality inversions, information produced after consumption, or impossible propagation-speed claims.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| PLAUSIBILITY-001 | 0.68 | HIGH | Engine specific-power constraint is unitless, so architectural feasibility is not physically grounded. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-002 | 0.58 | MEDIUM | Acceleration compliance is inferred from an oversimplified power-to-mass surrogate rather than a feasible longitudinal dynamics model. |
| PLAUSIBILITY-003 | 0.60 | MEDIUM | Braking requirement lacks the causal model needed to show brake torque can produce the claimed deceleration. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.62
